# Pre-F-212 Runtime Baseline (T006)

# Generated: 2026-04-25
# Method: 5 timed executions of scripts/extract-infographic-data.py
# Target: ~/Projects/second-brain-mcp/docs/security/2026-04-23T23-02-25/ (reference dataset)
# Template: executive-architecture
# Env: SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=1700000000
# Python: cpython-3.12.11 (uv-managed)
# Measurement: /usr/bin/time -p, "real" wall-clock seconds

# === Per-run wall-clock (seconds) ===
run_1_real = 0.08
run_2_real = 0.03
run_3_real = 0.03
run_4_real = 0.03
run_5_real = 0.03

# === Statistics ===
mean_seconds_all_runs = 0.040
mean_seconds_excluding_first = 0.030
median_seconds = 0.030

# === SC-212-8 gate (≤10% regression) — ORIGINAL READING (deprecated; see Wave 3 re-baseline below) ===
# Mean baseline: 40 ms (all 5 runs)  |  30 ms (warm runs only — exclude run_1 first-time IO)
# Post-F-212 acceptable mean: ≤44 ms (all-runs basis)  |  ≤33 ms (warm-runs basis)
# Recommended comparison basis: warm-runs only (excluding first-run cache miss)

sc_212_8_gate_warm_seconds_max = 0.033
sc_212_8_gate_all_seconds_max = 0.044

# === Notes (original) ===
# - Run 1 is consistently 2-3x slower due to filesystem cache and Python startup.
# - Subsequent runs stabilize at ~30 ms.
# - The reference dataset has 9 qualifying findings across 3 layers; F-212 L2's Largest
#   Remainder Method allocation (4-step calculation) is O(layers) — negligible additional cost.
# - F-212 L3's _build_flow_edges (sort O(N log N), N=number of data_flows) and _build_clusters
#   (sort O(N log N), N=number of trust_boundaries) are also negligible at production scale.
# - 10% regression headroom (3 ms warm / 4 ms all) is generous for the algorithmic additions.

# ============================================================================
# WAVE 3 RE-BASELINE (T020 finding, 2026-04-25) — supersedes the warm-runs gate above
# ============================================================================
# CONTROLLED A/B FINDING: The "30 ms warm-runs" baseline above was a
# measurement-session artefact, not a stable property of the code. Re-running
# the SAME baseline code (commit 2f48bc0, predates F-212) on the same hardware
# today produces 50-55 ms warm-runs — identical-or-slower than the post-F-212
# code path. /usr/bin/time -p reports two-decimal seconds (10 ms buckets), so
# the categorical jump from "0.03" to "0.04" or "0.05" reflects environment
# drift well below the methodology's resolution floor.
#
# A/B EVIDENCE (2026-04-25, controlled):
#   Wave 1 baseline code (2f48bc0, pre-F-212): 0.12 / 0.05 / 0.05 / 0.05 / 0.07
#     -> mean warm-runs = 55 ms, mean all-runs = 68 ms
#   Post-F-212 code (Wave 3 with caching fix):  0.10 / 0.04 / 0.04 / 0.04 / 0.06
#     -> mean warm-runs = 45 ms, mean all-runs = 56 ms
#
# CONCLUSION: There is no measurable F-212 performance regression. The
# post-F-212 code is, if anything, marginally faster than the Wave 1 baseline
# under today's measurement conditions.
#
# RE-BASELINED VALUES (effective for SC-212-8 gate evaluation):
rebaselined_warm_seconds_baseline = 0.055      # Wave 1 code, today's hardware
rebaselined_all_seconds_baseline  = 0.068      # Wave 1 code, today's hardware
rebaselined_sc_212_8_gate_warm_seconds_max = 0.061   # +10% of 55 ms = 60.5 ms
rebaselined_sc_212_8_gate_all_seconds_max  = 0.075   # +10% of 68 ms = 74.8 ms

# Post-F-212 (Wave 3, 45 ms warm / 56 ms all) is BELOW both gates.
# SC-212-8 gate disposition: PASS under the re-baselined comparison.
#
# NOTES on the re-baseline:
# - The 16-μs saving from caching `_qualifying_per_layer` (Wave 3 perf-fix
#   commit) is correct on its own terms but BELOW /usr/bin/time -p's 10 ms
#   resolution floor. It is retained as defensive perf hygiene.
# - The re-baseline was NOT motivated by a desire to pass the gate — it was
#   motivated by the controlled A/B showing the original 30 ms reading was
#   a measurement-session artefact. The architect-recommended path is
#   "re-baseline under stable load, accept 10 ms quantization as the floor"
#   (path 1 of 3 in `.aod/results/senior-backend-engineer-t020-fix.md`).
# - For Wave 4 / future regression tracking, ms-precision tooling
#   (time.monotonic() or hyperfine) is recommended if sub-10ms regressions
#   need to be detectable.
